key_points: Population refers to the total number of people living in a particular area
Population is an important element in social studies because people develop and use resources
India has about 17 percent of the world’s population
India occupies only about 2.4 percent of the world's land area
Population distribution in India is uneven across different regions
The Northern Plains have very high population density due to fertile land and water availability
Mountain regions and deserts have sparse population due to harsh climate and difficult terrain
Population density refers to the number of persons living per square kilometre of land area
Population growth refers to the increase in population over time
Birth rate, death rate and migration are the main factors affecting population change
examples: Example of densely populated region: Northern Plains of India
Example of sparsely populated region: Himalayan Mountains
Example of desert region with low population: Thar Desert
Example of a highly populated state: Uttar Pradesh
Example of urban migration: People moving from villages to cities for jobs
Example of health issue among adolescents: Anaemia
formulas: Population density = Total population / Total land area
Population growth = Increase in population over time
Natural increase = Birth rate − Death rate
Migration = Movement of people from one place to another
exam_tips: Remember the definition of population and population density
Understand the reasons for uneven population distribution in India
Learn the difference between birth rate, death rate and migration
Know the age group of adolescents (10–19 years)
Remember the importance of National Population Policy 2000 in controlling population growth
